headline: Case Studies
description: A curated selection of real QA engineering engagements. Each project demonstrates a specific discipline — automation, manual testing, API validation, accessibility, or AI system QA.

projects.title: SauceDemo E2E Selenium Automation Suite
projects.summary: Designed and implemented a production-ready Selenium + Pytest automation framework using Page Object Model architecture, layered fixtures, and GitHub Actions CI/CD.
whatCovered: Built Selenium + Pytest framework from scratch using Page Object Model
Created a layered fixture architecture to prevent stale sessions
Structured six dedicated page objects for clean test-layer separation
Integrated Allure reporting and GitHub Actions for automated CI execution
impact: Eliminated 100% of implicit wait usage — zero flaky tests in CI pipeline
Reusable authenticated sessions drastically cut execution time
Regression suite runs end-to-end automatically on every push via GitHub Actions
POM architecture makes adding new tests fast with minimal boilerplate

sections.title: Project Overview
sections.content: A production-grade UI test automation framework built with Selenium WebDriver, pytest, and Allure Reports. The goal was not just to test SauceDemo, but to build a framework mirroring enterprise standards that solves common scaling problems like locator duplication, flaky sessions, and slow execution.

sections.title: Architecture Decisions (Why POM?)
sections.content:
I did not start with the Page Object Model. Initially, I wrote direct Selenium scripts with inline locators. This quickly became unmaintainable as test coverage increased across login, cart, and checkout flows.

The main failure points of the initial approach were locator duplication across files, a high breakage rate when the UI changed, and no clear separation between test logic and page interaction.

I intentionally refactored the entire suite to POM to centralize locators and actions. This drastically reduced code duplication and improved maintainability when adding new flows.

sections.title: Framework Structure
sections.content:
saucedemo-automation/
│
├── pages/                          ← Page Object Model layer
│   ├── login_page.py               ← Login page actions and locators
│   ├── inventory_page.py           ← Product listing page
│   ├── cart_page.py                ← Shopping cart page
│   ├── checkout_page.py            ← Checkout form page
│   ├── checkout_overview_page.py   ← Order summary page
│   └── checkout_complete_page.py   ← Order confirmation page
│
├── tests/                          ← Test suites
│   ├── test_login.py               ← Login functional + negative tests
│   ├── test_inventory.py           ← Product and cart badge tests
│   ├── test_cart.py                ← Cart management tests
│   ├── test_checkout.py            ← Form validation tests
│   └── test_complete_flow.py       ← Full e2e purchase flow
│
├── utils/
│   └── test_data.py                ← Centralized test data
│
├── conftest.py                     ← Fixtures: driver, logged_in_driver
├── pytest.ini                      ← pytest config + markers
└── requirements.txt                ← Project dependencies

challenges.challenge: Session leakage and slow execution times due to improper teardown and repeating the login step for every single test.
challenges.solution: Implemented layered fixtures in `conftest.py` (`driver` → `logged_in_driver`). Scoped them properly and used `yield` to ensure stale sessions were destroyed. This provided a reusable authenticated session that drastically sped up execution.

sections.title: What Makes This Stand Out
items: Strict separation of concerns: tests never contain locators; page objects never contain assertions.
Layered fixtures ensure tests start in the exact state they need (fresh vs authenticated) without wasting execution time.
Fully integrated CI pipeline with step-level Allure reporting.

projects.title: MERN Chat Application — Full Manual QA Engagement
projects.summary: End-to-end manual QA engagement on a MERN stack chat application. Built a strict Requirements Traceability Matrix and reported 4 real defects including a Critical security vulnerability.
whatCovered: Designed 29 structured test cases covering auth, messaging, and real-time chat
Built a Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM) linking tests to requirements
Executed exploratory testing to uncover edge cases beyond written test cases
Reported 4 critical and high-severity defects with full reproduction steps
impact: Discovered a JWT session persistence vulnerability — session remained active post-logout
Identified a WebSocket zombie connection causing silent resource leaks
Achieved 100% requirement coverage across all tested modules via RTM
Delivered a complete artifact suite: test plan, test cases, RTM, and bug reports

sections.title: Project Overview
sections.content: A structured manual testing engagement covering the core functional, security, API, and real-time requirements of a fullstack MERN chat application. I treated this as a strict enterprise QA assignment, producing a complete documentation suite and discovering 4 real, unscripted defects.

sections.title: Design Decisions (Why an RTM?)
sections.content:
I intentionally built a full Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM) to demonstrate structured QA thinking. While arguably overkill for a simple chat app, without the RTM, there was a high risk of missing edge cases like message ordering or connection handling.

The RTM ensured that every functional requirement (send/receive messages, reconnect logic) was mapped to specific test cases, allowing me to identify gaps in coverage early and prove 100% requirement coverage.

sections.title: Documentation Structure
sections.content:
chat-app-manual-testing/
├── 01-requirements/requirements.md  ← 21 functional/technical requirements
├── 02-test-plan/test-plan.md        ← Strategy, scope, risks, entry/exit criteria
├── 03-test-design/test-cases.md     ← 29 detailed test cases
├── 04-defects/defect-log.md         ← 4 defects with reproduction steps & evidence
└── 05-rtm/rtm.md                    ← REQ → TS → TC → BUG traceability matrix

challenges.challenge: Suspected a connection cleanup issue due to increasing latency after multiple login/logout cycles, but the bug was completely invisible in the UI.
challenges.solution: Approached it from a system-behavior perspective. I opened Chrome DevTools, navigated to the Network tab, filtered for WebSocket connections, and observed multiple active connections stacking up instead of a single persistent one. Old sockets were not closing on logout (a Zombie Connection leak).

challenges.challenge: A critical JWT session vulnerability required precise timing and steps to reproduce without developer pushback.
challenges.solution: Documented exact exploratory steps: copying the JWT from cookies before logout, logging out, and manually re-inserting the token to successfully regain full Dashboard access, proving the server was not invalidating sessions.

sections.title: What Makes This Stand Out
items: Discovered a highly technical 'Zombie WebSocket' bug by inspecting network traffic, proving debugging skills beyond surface-level UI testing.
Maintained strict end-to-end traceability (REQ → TS → TC → BUG).
Demonstrated process discipline identical to enterprise QA environments.

rows: Case-variant emails bypass duplicate account check

1. Register with User@test.com
2. Register again with user@test.com
3. Both accounts are successfully created.
Image messages fail to send in chat

1. Open chat window
2. Attach image via UI
3. Click Send
4. Server returns error.
WebSocket connection stays open after logout (Zombie Connection)

1. Login to dashboard
2. Open DevTools > Network (WS)
3. Click Logout
4. Socket remains open and active.

projects.title: Wikipedia WCAG 2.1 Accessibility Audit
projects.summary: Conducted a structured manual accessibility audit of Wikipedia's core pages against WCAG 2.1 Level AA, focusing on real user impact rather than just technical compliance.
whatCovered: Audited against WCAG 2.1 Level A and AA success criteria across core page types
Tested keyboard-only navigation and focus order across search, article, and edit flows
Evaluated color contrast ratios and heading hierarchy using accessibility tools
Reviewed ARIA landmark usage, alt text completeness, and screen reader compatibility
impact: Documented accessibility gaps across search, article, and edit page user flows
Identified focus management issues directly affecting keyboard-only users
Produced a structured audit report with WCAG criterion reference and severity per finding
Prioritized issues based on actual user impact rather than strict rule violations

sections.title: Project Overview
sections.content: A structured WCAG 2.1 Level AA accessibility audit of a high-traffic Wikipedia article. The audit combined automated scanning (WAVE, Lighthouse) with manual keyboard navigation and screen reader testing to evaluate real-world compliance.

sections.title: Design Decisions & Trade-offs
sections.content:
WCAG guidelines are famously strict, but real-world impact varies. A major trade-off during this audit was deciding how to classify severity.

For example, some contrast issues technically violated WCAG thresholds but remained highly readable for most users. Instead of blanket-reporting everything as a critical failure, I classified severity based strictly on user impact, not just guideline violation. This avoids over-reporting low-impact issues and keeps the engineering focus on critical accessibility barriers.

challenges.solution: Reserved 'High Severity' strictly for issues that outright blocked navigation or screen reader usability (e.g., empty links, missing alt text on functional images, or focus traps).

Classified minor contrast violations or spacing issues with low usability impact as Medium or Low. For example, a search dropdown that didn't close immediately was marked Medium because it overlapped content but didn't break the core user flow.

sections.title: What Makes This Stand Out
items: Pragmatic approach to accessibility: prioritizing real human impact over robotic rule enforcement.
Combined manual keyboard testing with automated tools, recognizing that tools alone cannot determine logical focus order.
Produced a clear, developer-ready issue report mapping directly to WCAG 2.1 criteria.
